briefs.working_title: Your New Lakewood Ranch Home Came With Tubes in the Walls. Here's What They Do — and Don't — Protect You From.
secondary_kws: tubes in the wall pest control
briefs.thesis: Your builder included a pest control system in your walls. Here's what it actually treats, what it can't touch (drywood termites, subterranean termites, rodents, lawn/turf pests, mosquitoes), and the one thing nobody tells you at closing: it only works with one company's key.
outline: What Taexx actually is (tubes, exterior port, 10–15 zones — neutral, accurate description)
What it treats reasonably well (wall-void crawling insects) — be fair; credibility requires it
What it does NOT cover — lead with termites: HomeTeam's own knowledge center states Taexx 'is not a deterrent for termites.' In SWFL that's the headline risk (link Waves drywood/subterranean content)
The proprietary key: HomeTeam's FAQ states the port box is tamper-resistant, locked, and the key 'is not commercially available' — what that means for your service options (bridge to A2)
What a complete first-year protection plan looks like for a new SWFL build (bridge to A3)
FAQ block (5–7 Qs mirroring secondary KWs)

briefs.working_title: Do You Have to Use HomeTeam? (Taexx lock-in)
secondary_kws: do i have to use hometeam pest defense
briefs.intent: decision/defector BOFU — searcher holding a HomeTeam renewal notice
briefs.thesis: Short answer per HomeTeam's own materials: the port requires a proprietary key that isn't commercially available, so the in-wall system is effectively single-vendor. Longer answer: you don't need the tubes to have excellent pest control — here's what conventional exterior/interior IPM service covers in a new SWFL build, and why the tubes-vs-no-tubes question matters less than the termite question.
outline: The direct answer (cite the key/lock language; no editorializing — the fact does the work)
What you're actually deciding: tube service vs. full IPM service (coverage comparison table)
Registering the system 'doesn't commit you to any service contracts' (their language) — what your obligations actually are vs. aren't as a new owner
The termite gap, again (one paragraph, link A1 + C1)
Switching checklist: what to ask, what to document, no-contract option
FAQ block

Month-by-month year-1 plan, SWFL-specific. Field notes: ghost ants on slab edges, midges from new retention ponds, sod pests in fresh St. Augustine.

Your builder's termite pre-treat: what bifenthrin pre-slab treatment is, how long protection actually lasts, what voids it
Months 1–3 / 4–8 / 9–12 plan (interior, exterior, lawn establishment — link new sod care post)

briefs.working_title: PILLAR: How to Cancel a Pest Control Contract in Florida
secondary_kws: how to get out of a pest control contract
briefs.intent: BOFU defector, brand-agnostic. PILLAR — B2-B5 link up to this.
briefs.thesis: The honest owner's guide to pest control contracts in Florida: how auto-renewal clauses work, what cancellation fees are typical, your rights, the exact cancellation playbook (written notice, confirmation number, card-on-file hygiene), and what no-contract service looks like instead.
outline: Why pest control contracts auto-renew (the retention economics, stated plainly)
Florida's automatic-renewal disclosure law — VERIFY: pull current text of Fla. Stat. § 501.165 and confirm applicability to pest service contracts; do not assert until verified
The cancellation playbook (works for any brand): written notice, dates, confirmation, dispute path, BBB/FDACS complaint routes
Typical fees by brand (sourced table — Aptive $199 per ConsumerAffairs; TruGreen no early-termination fee but owes for delivered treatments per LegalClarity; verify each at publish)
Arbitration clauses and class-action waivers — what you agreed to (TruGreen example, sourced)
The alternative: what month-to-month, guarantee-backed service looks like (Waves model, link guarantee)
FAQ block
verify_notes: Statute verification mandatory before any legal claim ships — writer must verify Fla. Stat. § 501.165 applicability and cite it, or omit the claim entirely.

briefs.working_title: How to Read a Termite Bond Before You Sign It
briefs.primary_kw: what does a termite bond cover
briefs.intent: high-ticket decision MOFU/BOFU; feeds WDO/real-estate pipeline
briefs.thesis: A termite bond is a contract, and the difference between a retreatment-only bond and a repair bond can be a six-figure difference. Clause-by-clause walkthrough — exclusions, inspection obligations (theirs and yours), transfer terms, price-escalation language — anchored by the largest cautionary tale in the industry.
outline: Retreatment vs. repair bonds (the table that decides everything)

The case study, sourced: Alabama AG's $60M Terminix settlement — pattern of collecting annual premiums while failing to perform initial treatments, annual inspections, and no-cost retreatments; large price increases with no added service; consumers stuck between old and new companies over 'old damage.' State plainly the settlement context, that processes were established for consumer claims, and that Terminix admitted no wrongdoing per contemporaneous coverage.

The 9 clauses to read before signing (exclusions: moisture/conducive conditions, drywood carve-outs; annual inspection requirements; renewal escalators; transferability — link existing termite bond post)

Proof of treatment: what documentation to demand from ANY company — and how Waves does it (GPS-verified visits, tamper-evident hash-chained service reports, FDACS-13645-spec WDO inspections). 'Ask your termite company to show you proof' is a CTA only we survive.
Buying or selling? The WDO bridge (link)
FAQ block
verify_notes: Settlement description must track AG release language exactly; include the no-admission-of-wrongdoing line.

briefs.working_title: Termite Damage Claim Denied: Why, and How to Protect Yourself
secondary_kws: termite company won't pay for damage
briefs.thesis: The most common denial is 'that's old damage' — and per the Alabama record, consumers get caught exactly there: the new company calls it old, the old company says no contract. The defense is documentation. What to gather, how to dispute, how to escalate (FDACS complaint path), and how to never be in this position again.
outline: The 'old damage' trap, sourced (Alabama coverage quote pattern)
Your dispute playbook: bond language, inspection records, photo timeline, independent WDO report, FDACS complaint route
What an independent second-opinion inspection gives you (and what ours documents: timestamped, hash-chained, FDACS-13645)
Prevention: the documentation standard to demand from day one (links C1)

briefs.thesis: National lawn programs run on route-density calendars; St. Augustine in SWFL runs on soil tests, county blackout law, and disease windows read off weather data. briefs.working_title: Sensor Subscriptions vs. Actual Inspections: What 'Smart' Termite Protection Really Means
briefs.thesis: Sensor-based monitoring is a real technology with real limits: a sensor detects activity at the sensor. What it can't replace is a trained inspection of conducive conditions, and what matters more than either is documentation you can verify. Fair walkthrough of what TurnerGuard-style systems do, what square-footage subscription pricing means over 5 years, and the verification standard to hold any company to — sensors or not.
outline: How in-ground/sensor monitoring actually works (fair, technical)

What sensors can't see: drywood termites entirely (no soil contact), conducive conditions, interior evidence — the inspection layer. Drywood limitation framed as a property of soil-based systems generally, not a Turner defect.
Subscription math: square-footage monthly pricing over a 5-year horizon vs. inspection + targeted treatment (sourced to Turner's published plan structure; snapshot)
The Sunstate→Turner note: acquisitions and what happens to legacy bonds/warranties (factual; 'ask these 3 questions if your company was acquired')
The verification standard regardless of vendor: timestamped, tamper-evident reports; what ours look like
FAQ block
verify_notes: All TurnerGuard capability claims quoted from their pages.
